---
title: "Modules, Compilers, and Your First C Program on Lonestar6"
subtitle: "From environment setup to a compiled executable"
author: "Richard Herrington"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    code-overflow: wrap
    incremental: false
    preview-links: auto
    controls: true
    navigation-mode: linear
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-background-image: unt.png
  data-background-size: 23%
  data-background-position: 2% 2%
---

## This is Worshop 3 in the course sequence

- Workshop 1: first login, basic Linux, first SLURM job
- Workshop 2: monitor, inspect, cancel, debug
- **Workshop 3: modules, compilers, first C program**
- Later: MPI/OpenMP, containers

## Workshop outcomes

By the end of this session, you should be able to:

- explain what compilation does
- load a compiler module
- check which compiler you are using
- compile a small C program with `gcc`
- run the executable
- prepare compiled code for a SLURM job

## Mental model: from source code to executable

```text
source code (.c)
      ↓
compiler (gcc)
      ↓
executable (binary file)
      ↓
run on LS6
```

Key idea:

- source code is human-readable
- executables are what the system runs

## Why compilation matters in HPC

On HPC systems:

- software is often not globally installed
- compiler versions matter
- toolchains affect behavior
- performance depends on how code is built

## Two compilers you will hear about

- `gcc` → C
- `g++` → C++

Today we focus on **C with `gcc`**.

## Modules first, then compilation

Before compiling, load a compiler environment:

```bash
module avail
module load gcc
module list
```

Key idea:

- on clusters, software is often environment-based

## Check what compiler you are actually using

```bash
which gcc
gcc --version
```

Why this matters:

- confirms the command exists
- confirms which version is active
- avoids guessing

## Your first C program

Create a file:

```bash
nano hello.c
```

Add:

```c
#include <stdio.h>

int main() {
    printf("Hello from C on LS6\n");
    return 0;
}
```

## Compile the program

```bash
gcc hello.c -o hello
```

What that means:

- `hello.c` = source file
- `-o hello` = name of the executable

## Run the executable

```bash
./hello
```

Expected result:

```text
Hello from C on LS6
```

## Inspect what you created

```bash
ls -l
```

You should now see at least:

- `hello.c`
- `hello`

Key idea:

- source file and executable are different files

## Mental model: what just happened?

You:

- wrote source code
- used a compiler
- created an executable
- ran the executable from the shell

That is the core compiled workflow.

## Add a common optimization flag

```bash
gcc hello.c -o hello -O2
```

Useful levels:

- `-O0` = no optimization
- `-O2` = standard choice
- `-O3` = more aggressive

For beginners, `-O2` is a good default.

## Very small C++ contrast

```bash
g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp
```

Key idea:

- same overall workflow
- different compiler for C++

We are not going deep into C++ today.

## Automating builds with a Makefile

Create a file named `Makefile`:

```make
all:
	gcc hello.c -o hello
```

Then run:

```bash
make
```

Why this matters:

- avoids retyping build commands
- scales to larger projects

## Compiling on login nodes, running on compute nodes

Good rule of thumb:

- **compile on login nodes** for small builds
- **run heavier workloads through SLURM**

That keeps the workflow aligned with the earlier workshops.

## Running compiled code through SLURM

Example `run.slurm`:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J compile_test
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH -n 1
#SBATCH -t 00:05:00
#SBATCH -p normal

./hello
```

Submit with:

```bash
sbatch run.slurm
```

## Common beginner failures

- forgot `module load gcc`
- typo in source code
- forgot `-o hello`
- tried to run from the wrong directory
- executable is missing because compile failed

## Debug checklist for compilation

Check in this order:

```bash
module list
which gcc
ls -l
gcc hello.c -o hello
./hello
```

If compilation fails, read the compiler message carefully.

## Mini exercise

1. create `hello.c`
2. load `gcc`
3. compile with `gcc hello.c -o hello`
4. run `./hello`
5. recompile with `-O2`
6. submit through SLURM if time allows

## Instructor demo options

Choose one short demo:

- compile successfully from scratch
- introduce a small typo in C code
- forget to load the compiler module
- run from the wrong directory

## What you accomplished

You can now:

- load a compiler module
- verify your toolchain
- compile a C program
- run the executable
- connect compiled code to SLURM

## Bridge to Workshop 4

**Next workshop: Parallel Computing with MPI and OpenMP on LS6**

That continues the same course arc:

- environment awareness
- scheduler literacy
- software building
- then parallel execution

## Quick reference

```bash
module avail
module load gcc
module list
which gcc
gcc --version
gcc hello.c -o hello
gcc hello.c -o hello -O2
./hello
make
sbatch run.slurm
```
